_note: Pre-computed by running ai-stepwise.py locally. Regenerate by re-running the script and copying the printed numbers here.
prompt:
You are helping a predictive-analytics student improve a forward-stepwise linear regression on the California Housing dataset. The target is MedHouseVal (median house value, $100k units). The available raw columns are exactly: MedInc, HouseAge, AveRooms, AveBedrms, Population, AveOccup, Latitude, Longitude.

Propose exactly 10 engineered features that may carry predictive power.
Rules:
  * Use ONLY the raw columns listed above.
  * Each feature must be a deterministic function of the raw columns.
  * Briefly justify each suggestion (one sentence).
Respond as JSON: a list of objects, each with keys "name", "formula", "rationale".

response.name: rooms_per_person
response.rationale: Living space per occupant — strong housing-quality signal.

response.name: bedrooms_per_room
response.rationale: Bedroom share; lower values indicate larger living rooms / luxury.

response.name: log_medinc
response.rationale: Income effects on price are typically concave.

response.name: medinc_sq
response.rationale: Captures convex high-income premium that linear MedInc misses.

response.name: log_population
response.rationale: Population is right-skewed; log stabilizes the relationship.

response.name: pop_per_occup
response.rationale: Approximate household count in the block — neighborhood density.

response.name: dist_to_LA
response.rationale: Proximity to Los Angeles strongly drives California prices.

response.name: dist_to_SF
response.rationale: Proximity to San Francisco is a second major price gradient.

response.name: medinc_x_houseage
response.rationale: Old homes in rich areas (historic neighborhoods) price differently.

response.name: lat_x_long
response.rationale: Cheap nonlinear location term that flexibly captures geography.

A.name: Baseline stepwise
A.blurb: Forward stepwise on the 8 raw features. The honest no-frills baseline.

B.name: AI-augmented stepwise
B.blurb: 8 raw features + 10 LLM-suggested engineered features. Domain knowledge curated by Claude.

C.name: Kitchen-sink stepwise
C.blurb: 8 raw + every pairwise product (28) + every square (8). Brute-force expansion of the search space.

interpretation.verdict: Model C wins outright on this split, but Model B got 4.3% closer to it than Model A while considering only 41% as many candidate features as C. The LLM's domain knowledge is doing real work — it found most of the predictive signal without brute-forcing every interaction — but on a dataset this size the kitchen-sink expansion still squeezes out additional accuracy.
